techniques.evidence_quote: the attacker focused on creating another layer of persistence by creating an IAM user, supdev, with a login profile that had the AdministratorAccess policy attached
One of the first actions was to call GetFederationToken... credentials generated are separate from the original compromised IAM identity, making it more difficult to track the attacker

the attacker navigated to the AWS SES service, potentially checking the capabilities of the SES service in the victim's AWS account, like whether it was in a sandbox, had configured sending limits, etc. AWS SES enumeration API calls used: GetAccount, ListEmailIdentities, ListIdentities, GetSendQuota

attack_chain.description: Attacker used a stolen or compromised long-term IAM access key (AKIA) to gain initial access to the victim AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: we observed an attacker targeting an AWS environment with a long-term access key (AKIA)

attack_chain.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
attack_chain.description: Attacker called GetFederationToken via STS using the compromised AKIA to generate short-lived federated credentials (named 'admin') with a wildcard policy, decoupling activity from the original identity to evade detection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: One of the first actions was to call GetFederationToken... credentials generated are separate from the original compromised IAM identity, making it more difficult to track the attacker

attack_chain.description: Attacker used GetSigninToken and ConsoleLogin to convert the federated temporary credentials into an AWS Console session, logging in as federated-user/admin without MFA.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker then generated an AWS Console sign-in link using GetSigninToken... eventName: ConsoleLogin... arn: arn:aws:sts::redacted:federated-user/admin

attack_chain.description: Attacker created a new IAM role named 'SupportAWS' with a trust policy allowing assumption from the attacker-controlled external AWS account (713521355166), establishing cross-account persistence backdoor.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker used their console access to create a new role named SupportAWS... They then attached a policy to that role that allows them to assume it from an attacker-controlled account 713521355166

attack_chain.description: Attacker attached the AWS managed AdministratorAccess policy to the SupportAWS role, granting it full administrative privileges.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker attached the policy AdministratorAccess to the AWSSupport role. This ensures the role will have the requisite privileges to achieve the attacker's objectives.

attack_chain.description: Attacker assumed the SupportAWS role from their external account (713521355166) as IAM user 'adminprod', using SwitchRole and AssumeRole to gain administrative access via cross-account role assumption.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker then assumed the role via the 713521355166 account, authenticated as an IAM user named adminprod... generating API calls like SwitchRole and AssumeRole

attack_chain.description: Operating as SupportAWS/adminprod, the attacker created a new IAM user 'supdev', created a console login profile for it, and attached AdministratorAccess, establishing a second independent persistence foothold.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker focused on creating another layer of persistence by creating an IAM user, supdev, with a login profile that had the AdministratorAccess policy attached

attack_chain.description: Attacker logged into the AWS Console as the newly created IAM user 'supdev' from multiple regions in a short time period, without MFA.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker then pivoted to the user supdev, accessing the console twice from two different regions in a short time period.

attack_chain.description: Attacker enumerated the victim's AWS SES service using API calls (GetAccount, ListEmailIdentities, ListIdentities, GetSendQuota) to assess sending capabilities, sandbox status, and sending limits.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker navigated to the AWS SES service, potentially checking the capabilities of the SES service in the victim's AWS account, like whether it was in a sandbox, had configured sending limits, etc. AWS SES enumeration API calls used: GetAccount, ListEmailIdentities, ListIdentities, GetSendQuota

attack_chain.description: The following day, the attacker used the original long-term access key to call GetAccount across multiple regions, indicating automated tooling scanning for SES capabilities.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The following day we observed the same IP attempting the GetAccount API call across multiple regions from the original long-term access key. This indicated the use of automated tooling on the behalf of the attacker.

initial_access_vector: Stolen or compromised long-term IAM access key (AKIA) used directly against the AWS API
persistence_mechanism: 1) Created IAM role 'SupportAWS' with trust policy allowing assumption from attacker-controlled external AWS account (713521355166); 2) Created IAM user 'supdev' with console login profile and AdministratorAccess policy attached
privilege_escalation: Attacker created role 'SupportAWS' and attached AdministratorAccess managed policy, then assumed it from external account to gain full admin rights; subsequently created admin IAM user 'supdev' for an additional escalated foothold
